svg.title: <size:18>Word Processor using Flyweight Pattern (Annotated for Interview)</size>

g.text: Word Processor using Flyweight Pattern (Annotated for Interview)
Trace: "User types 'hello hello'. 10 characters but only 4 unique CharacterStyle objects (h,e,l,o). Flyweight shares them. Massive memory savings."
+addCharacter(char, row, col, font, size, color, bold, italic) : void
+applyStyle(fromRow, fromCol, toRow, toCol, font, size, color) : void

Document is the entry point. It owns pages

This object is SHARED, not duplicated.
never changes. This is critical — if shared

+getStyle(font, size, color, bold, italic, underline) : CharacterStyle
-buildKey(font, size, color, bold, italic, underline) : String
"The single most important class in this design."
4. If not: create new CharacterStyle, store in
This is the Flyweight Pattern in action.
No matter how many characters use 'Arial 12pt
Black Normal', only ONE CharacterStyle object
exists in memory. All characters point to it."
"The Flyweight Pattern splits object state into two:"
Stored ONCE in CharacterStyle. Shared by many."
Stored in each Character. Unique per character."
SAME for hundreds of characters. Duplicating
memory. Share one object, store only the
"User selects text and makes it Bold.
applies new style (gets from Factory — shared!).
Each formats rows differently within page width."
"User types text. Each character has a position
caches unique styles — 50,000 characters share ~20 style objects.
The Flyweight is the star — explain the intrinsic/extrinsic
Document -> Page -> Row -> Character (extrinsic: char + position)
Character holds pointer to shared style
CharacterStyle: font, size, color, bold, italic (intrinsic, immutable)
Factory: getStyle() returns cached object or creates new one
ApplyStyle / InsertChar / DeleteChar commands with undo
50,000 chars, 20 unique styles: WITHOUT Flyweight = 5 MB. WITH Flyweight = 400 KB. 92% savings.
